title: Pre-failure response spectra predict finite-amplitude fragility
abstract: Failure theories often identify a single leading route to failure: the most unstable mode, weakest link, minimum-action escape path, or optimal perturbation. Yet finite-amplitude susceptibility depends not only on the nearest route but on how much of perturbation space lies near dangerous directions. We cast this distinction as a fragility problem: for each perturbation direction, the failure distance is the smallest amplitude that crosses a prescribed boundary, and the fragility curve is the fraction of directions that fail below a given amplitude. Measuring this curve directly requires nonlinear trials over many directions; instead, we show that it is predicted, before any failure occurs, by the tail of a single pre-failure quantity: the boundary-normalized fragility gain computed from the linearized response. The breadth of the associated response spectrum sets how many near-dangerous pathways coexist beyond the strongest direction. We demonstrate the mechanism in a high-dimensional nonlinear non-normal network with the strongest directional gain held fixed: the system with broader response-channel breadth has a larger nonlinear fragility curve, isolating breadth from the worst direction. An independent scalar test in deterministic traffic breakdown confirms the predicted sign: response breadth lowers calibrated jam thresholds once the strongest response is matched, with residual margins screening but never reversing the effect. Response-spectrum breadth thus emerges as a pre-failure coordinate for finite-amplitude fragility beyond the strongest path.
summary: This work introduces the concept of 'finite-amplitude fragility,' arguing that system failure susceptibility depends not just on the worst-case path but on the volume of near-dangerous perturbations. The authors show that this volume dependence, or 'breadth,' can be predicted using only linear response diagnostics, providing a powerful, pre-failure diagnostic tool for complex nonlinear systems.
main_problem: The paper distinguishes between failure susceptibility determined by the single most unstable mode versus that dependent on the volume of nearly dangerous perturbations (finite-amplitude fragility).
main_result: The breadth of the response spectrum, quantified by measures like channel min-entropy, predicts the finite-amplitude fragility curve independently of the strongest directional gain.
method: The authors develop a pre-failure predictor based on the tail of the boundary-normalized fragility gain, validating it against nonlinear measurements in complex networks and traffic models.
model_or_system: The analysis uses high-dimensional nonlinear non-normal networks, a weakly nonlinear system ($\dot{x} = Ax +  eta x^{\circ 2}$), and a deterministic traffic breakdown model on a periodic ring.
key_observables: Fragility curve ($P_{	ext{fail}}(B)$), boundary-normalized fragility gain ($g(u)$), Response-Spectrum Breadth ($	ext{Sch}_{\min}, n_{	ext{ch}}^{	ext{eff}}$), and deterministic jam thresholds ($B_{th}$).
important_parameters: The strongest directional gain ($G_{\max, 	ext{dir}}$) is held fixed while varying the response-spectrum breadth.
main_assumptions: The primary predictor $Q_g(s)$ is shown to predict the nonlinear failure distance, though the linear approximation cost only provides bounds.
figures_summary: Figures compare concentrated vs. broadened channel weights, showing the broader system has a larger nonlinear fragility curve despite matched strongest directional gain. Traffic analysis shows threshold shifts correlating with response-path entropy.
paper_structure: The paper establishes the theoretical distinction between extremal failure and volume-dependent fragility, develops the linear predictor based on gain tails, validates this predictor in a branched network, and finally confirms the effect using a deterministic traffic flow model.
why_interesting: The concept of 'response-spectrum breadth' as a pre-failure coordinate is highly relevant for understanding robustness in complex, non-normal systems, which has parallels in analyzing stability in open quantum systems or coupled nonlinear oscillators.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Direct high-dimensional validation of response-spectrum fragility in a controlled nonlinear non-normal branched network. The geometry separates strongest-direction gain from response-channel breadth. Two stable N = 12 systems have matched strongest directional gain Gmax,dir but different channel-mode breadth. (a) Ranked response-channel weights show a concentrated system A and a broader system B. (b) Boundary-normalized gain g(u) predicts the nonlinear inverse failure distance 1/b∗(u) across sampled directions; the relation is strongly monotone though offset from the identity, consistent with nonlinear correction.
